issue_title: Flaky ran with py.test counts skipped tests as failing and reruns them
issue_body:
It looks like flaky (version 2.1.1) counts an intentionally skipped test as failed, and will rerun it up to `max_runs` times before reporting it as failed and allowing the test to be counted as skipped.

Code that displays the problem:
```python
import unittest, flaky
 
@flaky.flaky(max_runs=5)
@unittest.skip("example")
class SkipTestCase(unittest.TestCase):
    def test_skip(self):
        assert True
```

When run with `py.test`, this outputs

```
============================= test session starts ==============================
platform linux2 -- Python 2.7.8 -- py-1.4.27 -- pytest-2.7.0
rootdir: /home/jluke/Development/flaky-skip-demo, inifile: 
plugins: xdist, colordots, timeout, flaky
collected 1 items

test_flaky_skip.py s
===Flaky Test Report===

test_skip failed (4 runs remaining out of 5).
	<class 'builtins.Skipped'>
	example
	[<TracebackEntry /usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/_pytest/unittest.py:114>, <TracebackEntry /usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/_pytest/runner.py:468>]
test_skip failed (3 runs remaining out of 5).
	<class 'builtins.Skipped'>
	example
	[<TracebackEntry /usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/_pytest/unittest.py:114>, <TracebackEntry /usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/_pytest/runner.py:468>]
test_skip failed (2 runs remaining out of 5).
	<class 'builtins.Skipped'>
	example
	[<TracebackEntry /usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/_pytest/unittest.py:114>, <TracebackEntry /usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/_pytest/runner.py:468>]
test_skip failed (1 runs remaining out of 5).
	<class 'builtins.Skipped'>
	example
	[<TracebackEntry /usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/_pytest/unittest.py:114>, <TracebackEntry /usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/_pytest/runner.py:468>]
test_skip failed; it passed 0 out of the required 1 times.
	<class 'builtins.Skipped'>
	example
	[<TracebackEntry /usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/_pytest/unittest.py:114>, <TracebackEntry /usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/_pytest/runner.py:468>]

===End Flaky Test Report===

========================== 1 skipped in 0.01 seconds ===========================
```

I see no reason why flaky would try to rerun a skipped test multiple times.

issue_title: Django's LiveServerTestCase is not supported

issue_title: Guidline for handling client Oauth in web application
issue_body:
Hi there,

I have functionality on my website which allows Box users to integrate with our service and use our service from within Box. I have a callback URL "/box/callback" which will be called after a user authenticates with Box through our website. From here I store the access token and refresh token in our database for use with subsequent request to our server later. 

In order to make requests to box I am aware I need to create a client object with the oauth class passed to the object. Currently I am initiating an Oauth class with credentials by passing the refresh token and access token retrieved from the database directly into the init constructor. And then I pass the Oauth class into the Client class. However upon making requests using the client I receive an exception telling me there is no access token. This leads me to believe that I am creating the Oauth class incorrectly. 

How should I rebuild the Client class so I can make requests on behalf of my user?

Thanks

issue_title: why do the following models yield different results (training accuracy)?
issue_body:
I would like to fit a model, save & load it, and continue training.

For a given architecture and data set, my first model (without save & load) yields a training accuracy of
 

> [1] Train-accuracy=0.636530612244898
> [2] Train-accuracy=0.8794

```
devices = mx.cpu()
mx.set.seed(1337)
logger = mx.metric.logger$new()
model = mx.model.FeedForward.create(softmax, 
  X = train.x, y = train.y,
  ctx = devices,
  learning.rate = 0.03,
  momentum = 0.9,
  num.round = 2,
  array.batch.size = 100,
  eval.metric = mx.metric.accuracy,
  initializer = mx.init.uniform(0.07),

I used the following code:

```

model2 = mx.model.FeedForward.create(load$symbol,

issue_title: Must the last layer of mxnet model be output layers like Softmax, LinearRegression, etc?
issue_body:
Must the last layer of mxnet model be output layers like Softmax, LinearRegression, etc? I want to create a model where the output layer is actually a group of the outputs of convolution layers, however I bump into the problem of my data label not matching:

 Symbol.InferShapeKeyword argument name heatmap not found.

After my research on the issues that other posted, it seems that the name of the output layer should match the name of my output layer. However, Group layer does not have a name. My group layer consists of [c1, c2,..,c8] where each of the c's are output of a convolution layer, hence they are images.

All the examples on mxnet seems to have output layers defined in mxnet like Softmax and LinearRegression as the last layer.

Is Group as the last layer not supported?

For reference, the following is my code:

``` python
def createModel(nStack, nModules):
    inp = mx.symbol.Variable('data')

    cnv1_ = mx.symbol.Convolution(inp, num_filter = 64, kernel = (7, 7), stride = (2, 2), pad = (3, 3))
    cnv1 = mx.symbol.Activation(mx.symbol.BatchNorm(data = cnv1_), act_type = 'relu')
    r1 = residual(cnv1, 128, dim_match = False)
    pool = mx.symbol.Pooling(r1, kernel = (2, 2), stride = (2, 2), pool_type = 'max')
    r4 = residual(pool, 128, dim_match = True)
    r5 = residual(r4, 256, dim_match = False)

    inter = r5
    out = []

    for i in xrange(nStack):
        hg = hourglass(inter, 4, 256, nModules)
        for i in xrange(nModules):
            ll = residual(hg, 256, dim_match = True)
        ll = _conv_bn_relu(ll, num_filter = 256)
        tmpOut = mx.symbol.Convolution(ll, num_filter = 16, kernel = (1, 1), stride = (1, 1))
        out.append(tmpOut)

        if i < nStack:
            ll_ = mx.symbol.Convolution(ll, num_filter = 256, kernel = (1, 1), stride = (1, 1))
            tmpOut_ = mx.symbol.Convolution(tmpOut, num_filter = 256, kernel = (1, 1), stride = (1, 1))
            inter = inter + ll_ + tmpOut_

    group = mx.symbol.Group(out)

    return group

```

issue_title: Clojure's approx= test function has a weakness
issue_body:
## Description
The clojure framework's `approx=` test utility function (defined in `contrib/clojure-package/test/org/apache/clojure_mxnet/util_test.clj`) is unable to detect differences between arrays if they are of different sizes and the difference occurs in the excess.

For example, this returns true, but should return false:

```
(approx= 1e-9 [1 1 1] [1 1 1 9 9 9])
```

This means that tests that use `approx=` in the clojure test suite can incorrectly succeed.

## Minimum reproducible example
This is the localization net that I'm using:
```
def localization_net(image):
    # 40 * 40    
    data = conv_net(image, kernel=(3, 3), num_filter=16, name_num=1)
    data = conv_net(data, kernel=(3, 3), num_filter=16, name_num=2)
    # 36 * 36
    data = sym.Pooling(data, kernel=(2, 2), stride=(2, 2), pool_type='max', name="maxpool1")
    # 18 * 18
    data = conv_net(data, kernel=(3, 3), num_filter=32, name_num=3)
    data = conv_net(data, kernel=(3, 3), num_filter=32, name_num=4)
    data = conv_net(data, kernel=(3, 3), num_filter=32, name_num=5)    
    # 12 * 12
    data = sym.Pooling(data, kernel=(2, 2), stride=(2, 2), pool_type='max', name="maxpool2")
    # 6 * 6
    data = sym.Flatten(data)
    # 36*32
    data = sym.FullyConnected(data, num_hidden=500, name="fc1")
    data = sym.relu(data)
    data = sym.FullyConnected(data, num_hidden=100, name="fc2")
    data = sym.relu(data)
    data = sym.FullyConnected(data, num_hidden=6, name="fc3")
    return data
```

Here is the main model (an STN followed by a normal feedforward net):
```
def STN_example(image, batch_size, output_dims, out_name):
    theta = localization_net(image)
    img_crop = mx.sym.SpatialTransformer(image, theta, (24, 24), 'affine', 'bilinear')
    # 24*24
    # Run a small convnet on this
    data = conv_net(img_crop, kernel=(3, 3), num_filter=32, name_num="11")
    data = conv_net(data, kernel=(3 ,3), num_filter=32, name_num="12")
    # 20*20
    data = conv_net(data, kernel=(5, 5), num_filter=64, name_num="15")
    # 16*16
    data= sym.Pooling(data, kernel=(2, 2), stride=(2, 2), pool_type="max", name="maxpool_0")
    # 8*8
    data = sym.Flatten(data, name="flatten")
    data = sym.relu(sym.FullyConnected(data, num_hidden=1000), name="fc_0")
    data = sym.Dropout(data, p=0.2, name="dropout1")
    data = sym.relu(sym.FullyConnected(data, num_hidden=500), name="fc_1")

issue_title: Issue Installing mxnet for R on Ubuntu 14.04 ("cholmod.h" not found)
How to insert constant multiplier layer in my model definition
issue_body:
Hi,

From MXNet documentation, I follow the following strategy to define my model:

```python
data = mx.sym.Variable('data')
net = mx.sym.FullyConnected(data, name = 'fc1', num_hidden = 128)
net = mx.sym.Activation(net, name = 'relu1', act_type = "relu")
net = mx.sym.FullyConnected(net, name = 'fc2', num_hidden = 10)
net = mx.sym.SoftmaxOutput(net, name = 'softmax')
model = mx.mod.Module(net)

model.bind(data_shapes = nd_iter.provide_data, label_shapes = nd_iter.provide_label)
model.init_params()  
model.fit(nd_iter, num_epoch = 10)
```
where `nd_iter` is my MXNet *data iterator*.

Now let's say I want to insert a layer that multiplies all the values in that layer by a *constant* value (something achieved using `lambda` layers in *Keras*). From what I have understood till now, this can be achieved by inserting a *broadcasting* layer:

```python
multiplier = mx.symbol.Variable('constant', shape = (1,))
data = mx.sym.Variable('data')

net = mx.sym.FullyConnected(data, name = 'fc1', num_hidden = 128)
net = mx.sym.Activation(net, name = 'relu1', act_type = "relu")
net = mx.sym.broadcast_mul(lhs = net, rhs = multiplier, name = 'multiply') # new layer
net = mx.sym.FullyConnected(net, name = 'fc2', num_hidden = 10)
net = mx.sym.SoftmaxOutput(net, name = 'softmax')
model = mx.mod.Module(net)
```

My first question is, is this the proper (and most elegant) way to do it?
If yes, how do I assign value to the `multiplier` (let's say 10 for example)?

I understand that *values* can be assigned to *symbols* using
 `multiplier.bind(ctx = mx.cpu(),args = {'constant':10})`.

But how does this go into the entire model definition? 

Thanks in advance.

issue_title: A possible bug due to lacking synchronization after Copy
issue_body:
In the code of `src/operator/softmax_output-inl.h`, I think there might be a bug [in this line](https://github.com/dmlc/mxnet/blob/master/src/operator/softmax_output-inl.h#L160). This line aims to copy the contents in `label` to `workspace`. However, since in the case that `label` resides in gpu, the copy is asynchronous w.r.t the host, shouldn't there be a synchronize sentence after this line, e.g. `cudaStreamSynchronize(label.stream_)` to do synchronization? Otherwise, I think unexpected error may occur [here](https://github.com/dmlc/mxnet/blob/master/src/operator/softmax_output-inl.h#L163) where the content of `workspace` is used.

Any comments? Thanks a lot!

issue_title: Should not rewrite custom auth headers from _request_options
issue_body:
Hi, I'm using bravado for end-to-end checks. One use case is : 
1. Use `default_apikey` for every request
2. Create `new_apikey` using API
3. Check that requests with `new_apikey` applied work.

Currently to make this work one should wrap SwaggerClient/RequestsClient or create separate SwaggerClients for each api key. Reason is that `ApiKeyAuthenticator.apply` overwrites any custom auth headers from `_request_options`. 

It would be better if  `ApiKeyAuthenticator.apply` could  skip setting `request.headers[self.param_name]` if this header already exists.

I suppose similar thing is applicable to other Authenticator variations.

issue_title: No batching when yielding dicts in a fanout
issue_body:
Hello,

While converting our code to asynq I have came across a bug that causes batching to not happen when `yield`ing `dict`s in a fan out fashion.

I have repro code as well of this behavior in this gist: https://gist.github.com/LiraNuna/a90b6ac3cc820e07022787ab07204f9a

Notice when `yield`ing a `list` only a single batch is flushed but when `yield`ing a `dict`, each batch contains only a single item.

issue_title: makeTestRequest does not set PARENTS, breaking plone.subrequest
issue_body: plone.subrequest gets the app from the last item of the `PARENTS` attribute on the parent request. The resource registry uses `zope.globalrequest.getRequest` to get the current global request in `cookWhenChangingSettings` to use as the parent. This happens during GS import, which breaks importing the resource registries.

issue_title: yield from syntax error in _test_asyncio.py for 15.1.0
issue_body:
Hi!

I've downloaded the pyzmq-15.1.0.tar.gz source code and am building an RPM package for RHEL 7.x x86_64.  RHEL 7 ships with /usr/bin/python as python 2.7.5 (plus "vendor sauce" patches and backports).  The organization I work for is a strong proponent of OS packages for all add-on modules for scripting languages like Python, Perl, and Ruby, so we don't use tools like pip, cpan, or rubygem to directly install add-on modules.

The standard RPM machinery on RHEL has a process that runs to re-compile the python byte code automatically just before the binary RPM package is generated.  This script is called

```
    /usr/lib/rpm/brp-python-bytecompile
```

When that process runs over all the files in the build-root (a temporary location created as a staging area to collect all the files that should be part of the package) for pyzmq, it errors out with the following:

```
Bytecompiling .py files below /var/tmp/python-pyzmq-15.1.0-1.packager/usr/lib64/python2.7 using /usr/bin/python2.7
Compiling /var/tmp/python-pyzmq-15.1.0-1.packager/usr/lib64/python2.7/site-packages/zmq/tests/_test_asyncio.py ...
  File "/usr/lib64/python2.7/site-packages/zmq/tests/_test_asyncio.py", line 37
    yield from a.send(b'hi')
             ^
SyntaxError: invalid syntax
```

I'm not an expert Pythoneer, but based on my understanding, it appears that "yield from" was formalized as part of PEP-0380 and is essentially new syntax for Python 3.3 and later.  Am I correct in thinking that's why the python 2.7.5 from RHEL 7 is failing to (re)byte-compile that file?

Since this is in the tests subdirectory, I'm planning on working around the issue by removing that directory before issuing the

```
  python setup.py install --root=/var/tmp/python-pyzmq-15.1.0-1.packager
```

directive in the RPM spec file. See any problem with that approach?

issue_title: Using Non-AVX2/FMA Builds
issue_body:
Hello,

I am running through the standard set-up and was having some trouble on the final step. It appears to be a recurring issue, but the solution I believe is provided hasn't fixed the problem, which is why I'm assuming there is a bug. Very well may be me though so I've tried to describe every step.

I'm using a MacBook Pro 10.11.6 with a 2.4 GHz Intel Core 2 Duo Processor (incredibly old).

Tensorflow has not been installed (instruction don't seem to indicate it as a prerequisite).

Python is 2.7.

I first cloned the repository, but that doesn't seem to be important for what's next. Then I downloaded and unzipped the pre-trained model.

I set up a virtual environment for deepspeech using conda. Then I activated it and ran pip install deepspeech. After installation I ran:

deepspeech location_of_downloaded_pretrained_model.pg output_graph.pb location_of_sound.wav

This immediately got the "Illegal instruction: 4" and the code stopped. 

After a bit of searching it seems like the problem is AVX2/FMA as documented here (https://github.com/mozilla/DeepSpeech/issues/1057) and here (https://github.com/mozilla/DeepSpeech/issues/1023).

The consensus seemed to be to go to https://tools.taskcluster.net/index/artifacts/project.deepspeech.deepspeech.native_client.master/osx and select deepspeech-0.1.0-cp27-cp27m-macosx_10_10_x86_64.whl (since I'm running python 2.7 with an old Mac).

Then, I uninstalled the current deepspeech with:

pip uninstall deepspeech 

and ran:

pip install deepspeech-0.1.0-cp27-cp27m-macosx_10_10_x86_64.whl (while in a folder that contained it)

Seemed to work, but then I ran:

deepspeech location_of_downloaded_pretrained_model.pg output_graph.pb location_of_sound.wav

once more and it still gives me the error "Illegal instruction: 4" and the code stopped. 

Have I done something wrong here or is this still a bug?

issue_title: change readthedocs and versioning
issue_body:
Issue #146 came about because RTD is set up to build master tip. So any time we merge changes, it updates the docs. Thus the docs can get out of sync with the released version.

This is compounded by two other issues:

1. after we do a release, we don't then update the version to 0.x.y.dev0 or something like that (I do that with my projects) -- if we did this, then the docs would show that they apply to a different version than the released one
2. after we do a release, we don't then update RTD to build docs for that tag as well (I do this with my projects sometimes) -- if we did this, then users would see that the docs they're looking at apply to a different version and can then switch to the version they want to see

The place in RTD to update versions is here:

https://readthedocs.org/projects/mozilla-django-oidc/

But only people who have access to that can do it.

This issue can be fixed entirely by adjusting the release process. I don't know if there's a good way to automate either of them because I do them by hand, but that's probably worth looking into.

I think this is worth fixing process-wise.

How do we want to deal with this?

issue_title: Edge case: different metrics at creating and use
issue_body:
Hi,

Congratulations and thanks on the fantastic library! It's quite useful & fast (we use it for image matching, storing/querying vectors of image features).

I came upon edge case. It may be "too" edge case / strange to matter, but just in case:

```
from annoy import AnnoyIndex

VECTOR_LENGTH = 1
NMB_TREES = 1

t = AnnoyIndex(VECTOR_LENGTH, metric='euclidean')
t.add_item(0, [0.4])
t.add_item(1, [0.4])
t.build(NMB_TREES)
t.save('test.ann')

# this will work
u = AnnoyIndex(VECTOR_LENGTH, metric='euclidean')
u.load('test.ann')
print(u.get_nns_by_item(0, 1))

# this will raise 'Item index larger than the largest item index'
# notice the different metric
v = AnnoyIndex(VECTOR_LENGTH, metric='angular')
v.load('test.ann')
print(v.get_nns_by_item(0, 1))
```

A few findings about this:
- the problem goes away if indices 1 and 2 are used instead of 0 and 1.
- the problem appears when the index is created as `euclidean` but queried as `angular`; not the other way around

---

I came upon this when opening a small ~40Mb annoy index (1M entries, all 1-item vectors -- it's a special case) and forgetting to pass 'euclidean' to use the Annoy index. I kept getting `Segmentation fault: 11` errors, which led me to 1) figure out that I was wrong :-) 2) this edge case demonstration.

Cheers!

issue_title: DateIntervalParameter not ISO8601 interval?
issue_body:
Hello,

according to [the docs](https://luigi.readthedocs.org/en/latest/api/luigi.html?highlight=interval#luigi.DateIntervalParameter) `DateIntervalParameter` uses [ISO 8601 Time Interval notation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ISO_8601#Time_intervals).

However, there are four ways to express a time interval (according to wikipedia):

1. Start and end, such as `2007-03-01T13:00:00Z/2008-05-11T15:30:00Z`
2. Start and duration, such as `2007-03-01T13:00:00Z/P1Y2M10DT2H30M`
3. Duration and end, such as `P1Y2M10DT2H30M/2008-05-11T15:30:00Z`
4. Duration only, such as `P1Y2M10DT2H30M`, with additional context information

Looking at [the tests for `date_interval`](https://github.com/spotify/luigi/blob/master/test/date_interval_test.py#L130) it seems this is not what is implemented:

- `2015-01-01-2015-01-30` is considered a valid time interval
- this should probably be `2015-01-01/2015-01-30`

issue_title: Retry settings/logic at task level instead of at "global" scheduler level
issue_body:
Hi,

At the moment we have several luigi runs, for which we'd like to have different retry behaviour (some tasks should be retried every hour, some every ten minutes, for some tasks it's fine to be disabled after first failure, etc)

As far as I understand with current luigi we would have to use different configs for each run. And even then it is not possible to have different retry settings within the same run.

Wouldn't it make more sense to be able do define retry settings (retry delay, disable-num-failures, window, ...) or even retry related logic (e.g. custom back-off delay function, send warning mail, switching to a fallback task implementation, ...) at the task level instead of having this as global/scheduler properties?

I don't have a concrete proposal at the moment, but I just wanted to check with the maintainers if there are already ideas around this

issue_body:
To avoid #2871 this should be easy integrated (as an option) into `helpers/makerpm.sh` using Docker:

- If a local build is made, mount the folder inside Docker and run yum:
```bash
$ docker run --rm -v $(pwd):/io -t docker.io/centos:7 yum install /io/python-oq-engine*.noarch.rpm
```

- if a build is made on COPR download the package from it
```bash
$ docker run --rm -v $(pwd):/io -t docker.io/centos:7 "curl -sL https://copr.fedoraproject.org/coprs/gem/openquake-stable/repo/epel-7/gem-openquake-stable-epel-7.repo | tee /etc/yum.repos.d/gem-openquake-stable-epel-7.repo && \
yum install python-oq-engine-master python-oq-engine-worker 
```
`gem-openquake-stable-epel-7.repo` is when `-r` is set, otherwise it's `gem-openquake-epel-7.repo`

issue_title: VirtualDisk's capacityInKB attribute is deprecated in vSphere API 5.5 but is a required field in pyVmomi
issue_body:
In the [VMware vSphere API 5.5 spec for VirtualDisk](http://pubs.vmware.com/vsphere-55/index.jsp?topic=%2Fcom.vmware.wssdk.apiref.doc%2Fvim.vm.device.VirtualDisk.html), it states:

> Deprecated. As of vSphere API 5.5, use capacityInBytes.
> Capacity of this virtual disk in kilobytes. Information might be lost when actual disk size is rounded off to kilobytes.

Currently, I have this bit of code in a script:

    disk = pyVmomi.vim.vm.device.VirtualDisk()
    ...
    disk.capacityInKB = int(1048576 * size)  # 1 GiB == 1048576 KiB.

But when I replace `.capacityInKB` with `.capacityInBytes`, like so...

    disk = pyVmomi.vim.vm.device.VirtualDisk()
    ...
    disk.capacityInBytes = int(1073741824 * size)  # 1 GiB == 1073741824 B.

... the vSphere Web Client's Task Console reports a bit of an ambiguous error (device 0 being my disk):

> Invalid configuration for device '0'.

In poking around, I saw that when I create a VirtualDisk object using `.capacityInKB`, the object looks like this:

    (vim.vm.device.VirtualDisk) {
       ...
       capacityInKB = 1048576,
       capacityInBytes = <unset>,
    },

...and in creating a VirtualDisk object using `.capacityInBytes`, the object looks like this:

    (vim.vm.device.VirtualDisk) {
       ...
       capacityInKB = 0,
       capacityInBytes = 1073741824,
    },

In an attempt to create a workaround, I tried to set `.capacityInKB` to `None`, and this traceback appeared:

    ...
    /usr/lib/python3.4/site-packages/pyVmomi/VmomiSupport.py in __setattr__(self, name, val)
        521 
        522    def __setattr__(self, name, val):
    --> 523       CheckField(self._GetPropertyInfo(name), val)
        524       SetAttr(self, name, val)
        525 
    
    /usr/lib/python3.4/site-packages/pyVmomi/VmomiSupport.py in CheckField(info, val)
        890          # unset items
        891          if not (info.flags & F_OPTIONAL) and info.type is not object:
    --> 892             raise TypeError('Required field "%s" not provided (not @optional)' % info.name)
        893          return
        894       elif info.type is object:
    
    TypeError: Required field "capacityInKB" not provided (not @optional)

issue_title: GPG signatures for source validation
issue_body:
As we all know, today more than ever before, it is crucial to be able to trust
our computing environments. One of the main difficulties that package
maintainers of Linux distributions face, is the difficulty to verify the
authenticity and the integrity of the source code.

The Arch Linux team would appreciate it if you would provide us GPG signatures
in order to verify easily and quickly your source code releases.

**Overview of the required tasks:**
* Create and/or use a 4096-bit RSA keypair for the file signing.
* Keep your key secret, use a strong unique passphrase for the key.
* Upload the public key to a key server and publish the full fingerprint.
* Sign every new git commit and tag.
* Create signed compressed (xz --best) release archives
* Upload a strong message digest (sha512) of the archive
* Configure https for your download server

[GPGit](https://github.com/NicoHood/gpgit) is meant to bring GPG to the masses.
It is not only a shell script that automates the process of creating new signed
git releases with GPG but also comes with this step-by-step readme guide for
learning how to use GPG.

**Additional Information:**
* https://github.com/NicoHood/gpgit
* https://help.github.com/categories/gpg/
* https://wiki.archlinux.org/index.php/GnuPG
* https://git-scm.com/book/en/v2/Git-Tools-Signing-Your-Work
* https://www.qubes-os.org/doc/verifying-signatures/
* https://lkml.org/lkml/2016/8/15/445
* https://developers.google.com/web/fundamentals/security/encrypt-in-transit/why-https
* https://www.enigmail.net/index.php/en/

Thanks in advance.

issue_title: Upload to the Python Packaging Index
issue_body:
It would be incredibly convenient if you would upload this library to the Python Package Index.

In case you're not familiar with PyPI, all you have to do is register for an account at https://pypi.python.org/pypi, then check out duo_client_python and run

    python setup.py sdist register upload

This will ask for your PyPI username and password and remember them in ~/.pypirc, so that future uploads don't need to ask for it again.

Advantages of having your package published in PyPI are:
* people can find it
* people can install it by running `pip install duo_client`
* people who write libraries or applications can include it in their dependency lists by name

issue_title: `Get Cookies` should return cookies as objects, not string, and also log cookies
issue_body:
Currently `Get Cookies` returns a cookies as a string in format `cookie1=value1; cookie2=value2`. This isn't very useful if you need to actually handle all cookies somehow, and the return format isn't even documented anywhere. We should change the keyword to return cookies a list (or ordered dictionary) of CookieInformation objects that were introduced as part of #932.

Changing keyword return value is obviously backwards incompatible, so we cannot do it without a deprecation period. I propose that in v3.1 we add an argument like `return_objects=False` that can be used to explicitly change the return value. This allows users to opt-in if they want to get objects. In v3.2 we could then change the argument to `return_objects=True`, making it possible to opt-out if needed. And in v3.3 or later the whole argument can be removed. This issue only covers adding `return_objects=False` (or similar), changing and removing it later require separate issues later.

Another problem in `Get Cookies` is that it doesn't log anything. The beginning of the return value is logged automatically if the return value is assigned to a variable, but it would be better to log at least names and values explicitly.

issue_title: create requirements.txt without virtualenv?
issue_body:
hi! Is there a way to create a `requrirements.txt` file without creating a virtualenv? I'm trying to build a series of lambdas that each have separate `Pipfile`s.

After cding into each lambdas fodler, I'm running `pipenv lock -r | pip install -r /dev/stdin --target src`. Unfortunately, it creates a virtualenv per invocation which wastes time.

Is there an easy way to just translate the Pipfile.lock -> requirements.txt format without creating the virtualenv?

Thanks!

issue_title: Setting PIP_INDEX_URL results in two [[source]] sections
issue_body:
##### Describe your environment

1. OS Type macOS
1. Python version: `$ python -V` 3.6.4
1. Pipenv version: `$ pipenv --version` 9.1.0 (aka master)


##### Expected result

Setting PIP_INDEX_URL creates a Pipfile with a [[source]] set to the contents.


##### Actual result

Pipfile contains both the default section pointing to "https://pypi.python.org/pypi" and a section with my settings.


##### Steps to replicate

Since I don’t know a public PyPI index, let’s do a simpler thing:

`env PIP_INDEX_URL=https://pypi.python.org/pypi pipenv install requests`

results in

```toml
[[source]]

url = "https://pypi.python.org/pypi"
verify_ssl = true
name = "pypi"


[[source]]

url = "https://pypi.python.org/pypi"
verify_ssl = true
name = "pypi"
```

If they were different, the correct one would be at the first place.

issue_title: pipenv installs into .venv even though PIPENV_VENV_IN_PROJECT is not set
issue_body:
##### Issue description

If a folder exists called .venv, pipenv will automatically install into it even though PIPENV_VENV_IN_PROJECT is not set.

##### Expected result

I expected it to install into the global virtual environment location (usually `/home/moo/.local/share/virtualenvs/`)

##### Actual result

It installed into `./.venv/`


##### Extra information

It is worth to note what I am trying to achieve.

I am running Windows and VS Code installed in Windows. But I am also running wsl. What I am trying to do is to use wsl (bash) as my terminal in VS Code. There are few things to consider here.

1) I have installed python both in Windows and WSL/bash (Ubuntu).
2). I have set PIPENV_VENV_IN_PROJECT in Windows, but not in WSL.
2) The VS Code extension is configured like `"python.pythonPath": "${workspaceFolder}/.venv",`. For the VS Code extension to work, I need to execute `pipenv install` in Windows Powershell or cmd (i.e. in Windows). This is since VS Code is installed in Windows.
3) However, when I open the integrated terminal in VS Code I want to use WSL. As such, it cannot use .venv since everything installed there is Windows-specific. So I try to do `pipenv install` in the integrated terminal (running WSL/bash) in the hopes that it will install to the global venv location. This way, I could properly use both VS Code with the python extension, and at the same time use the integrated terminal to run things there as well.

issue_title: Failure to parse ipywidgets extras_require

```

    ':python_version<"3.3"' : ['ipython>=4.0.0,<6.0.0'],

issue_title: Extraneous Content in Pipfile.lock
issue_body:
It seems the opinion of the community is pretty unanimous:

### NOBODY WANTS LOCAL DEV OS JUNK IN `Pipfile.lock`

See #753

I don't know too many people that use Mac OS in production, so many people using Heroku or similar tools will have to deal with their app being in a perpetual [fugue state](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fugue_state).

I haven't yelled on GitHub before... but I also haven't come across a thread on an open source project where **everyone** wanted to remove a pretty bad feature from something and then the core maintainer popped in and said:

> going to remain, for now.

Then locked the thread to avoid hearing any other opinions on the matter (outside of the DPRK, we have an "Unsubscribe" button on the right side of the thread when we don't want the chatter).

issue_title: Assigning job priorty while scheduling
issue_body:
I am having a use case scenario where we are running new job for each task on same domain
I am handling this using `scrapyd` and `schedule.json` API
like 
```
curl http://localhost:6800/schedule.json -d project=myproject -d spider=somespider -d page_url=some_spdier_start_url
```

but there are some cases where, new jobs request needs to be served immediately ( soon after current jobs finishes) [i am using single process  per domain so have only one process running for any spider at any given time]

can we use priority queuing in scrapyd while scheduling job ?
https://github.com/scrapy/queuelib/blob/master/queuelib/pqueue.py
```
#something like
 curl http://localhost:6800/schedule.json -d project=myproject -d spider=somespider -d priority=1
#priority : {1- highest, 2 - high, 3 - normal, 4 - low, 5 - lowest } , default to 3 - normal
```

i noticed that this is implemented on Scrapinhub dashboard when we run a spider

issue_title: Add page to documentation explaining coordinate conventions in skimage
issue_body:
There's been a fair bit of recent confusion regarding coordinate systems in scikit-image. In particular, see #1142 (including [this comment](https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/pull/1142/files#r17855601)) and #1140. I suggest we adopt (at least) the following conventions:

* Numpy-style indexing is referred to as (row, col) or (dep, row, col): vertical axis first, then horizontal, origin is at top left. In the case of 3D, it's (somewhat unintuitively) depth first, then row, then column. Other possibilities are (row, col, ch) and (dep, row, col, ch).
* Matplotlib/Cartesian-style indexing has horizontal axis first, then vertical, then optionally "height", and should be referred to as xy or xyz. The origin is at the bottom-right.
* Another convention is horizontal, then vertical, but with the origin at the top left. This could (should?) be called (col, row).

We should strive to keep this consistent throughout the library to avoid confusion (see above issues), and we should add a page in the docs defining this, just as we do with data types and ranges.

issue_title: Implement quality metrics for image segmentation, classification and object detection
issue_body:
## Description

I believe that these days a vast majority of engineers and researchers in the field of image processing are working on image segmentation, classification and object detection problems. That said, I think that it could be benefitial to have some typical quality metrics implemented in `scikit-image`.
Of course, there are already some simple metrics in `scikit-learn`, but none of them are applicable as is to the problems mentioned above. Image processing-related problems are quite specific in that sense.

As a good starting point, I'd suggest to implement some of the metrics used in ImageNet Challenge (see both http://image-net.org/challenges/LSVRC/2012/index and http://image-net.org/challenges/LSVRC/2016/index) and/or PASCAL VOC (see both http://host.robots.ox.ac.uk/pascal/VOC/voc2007/htmldoc/index.html and http://host.robots.ox.ac.uk/pascal/VOC/voc2012/htmldoc/index.html).

Perhaps, we should consider adding several different algorithms for each problem (e.g. for object detection there is a brilliant article `B. Ozdemir, S. Aksoy, S. Eckert, M. Pesaresi, and D. Ehrlich, “Performance measures for object detection evaluation,” Pattern Recognit. Lett., vol. 31, no. 10, pp. 1128–1137, 2010.` with 3 different quality metrics to be used in different cases).

If you have any thoughts on this topic, please, do share.

P.S. Let this GitHub issue be an umbrella topic for the mentioned things.

issue_title: Registering Exception Views Through Cornice
issue_body:
Currently, Pyramid has a way to register exception views (http://docs.pylonsproject.org/projects/pyramid-cookbook/en/latest/pylons/exceptions.html#exception-views). The main drawback to using Pyramid's exception views is that you have to create a new Response object for each view. It would be nice if Cornice could provide a way to register exception views that pass in the existing response object so a new one doesn't have to be created. Something like this would be nice:

```python
@view_config(context=InvalidCreditCardError)
def bad_card(exc, request, response):
    response.errors.add('billing', 'formError', 'You entered an invalid credit card')
    response.errors.status = 501
    return response
```

Notice how the existing response object is passed into the function and errors and status can be easily set without creating a new Response object.

issue_title: WebSearch: fix search field drop-down behaviour
issue_body:
Originally on 2011-02-21

If people search for P1 P2 P3 in F, this query is always interpreted
as sending P1 P2 P3 to F, regardless of what is inside Pi.  The theory
was that if people selected F from the drop-down menu, the typed
search terms should be dispatched there.  However, if there is some Pi
of the form Fi:Xi, it would be much better to dispatch this search
unit to Fi instead.

This will fix queries like `ellis title:muon` in the `author`
drop-down menu, because it would effectively query for `ellis` in the
`author` index (as asked for by the drop-down) AND for `muon` in the
`title` index (as asked for by the colon syntax).

Note that this dispatching is what Invenio already does when
F=anyfield; basically we just need to do it for any F.  This should be
done by modifying `create_basic_search_units()`.

issue_title: session: remove CFG_WEBSESSION_DIFFERENTIATE_BETWEEN_GUESTS
issue_body:
```python
## CFG_WEBSESSION_DIFFERENTIATE_BETWEEN_GUESTS -- when set to 1, the session
## system allocates the same uid=0 to all guests users regardless of where they
## come from. 0 allocate a unique uid to each guest.
CFG_WEBSESSION_DIFFERENTIATE_BETWEEN_GUESTS = 0
```

In modern Flask time, if one wants to store some session related data for a guest user, should better do it in a cookie. This functionality had been implemented in the past in order to showcase certain Invenio functionalities (baskets, alerts) to guests users, without requiring them to login.

In modern time it is socially acceptable to require authentication to use user oriented functionalities of an online service.

So this RFC is to propose the removal of such configuration piece (thus simplifying the session/authentication/authorization stack).

Since the introduction of https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio/commit/552d1c4cca765911f5df100abb862e8b39d8862f the devserver console is full of messages like: ```python /home/jmartinm/.virtualenvs/pu-inspire2/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/jinja2/environment.py:966: RuntimeWarning: Convert string '/home/jmartinm/.virtualenvs/pu-inspire2/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/jinja2/environment.py:966: RuntimeWarning: Convert string '.eJxtkNEKgjAUhl9lCd1J0QN0YbnoohJmEGEiyy064Ka4U-Tbl2Fzhpf_9_0czjmJF9MdXR_JrawVxxkIEsRdyED4P665ko5po3V5KVzXRuuENHkNFUKpnYpDnSkapcYMm2o4rce2q0DJ_6JltvUEA1coABun1kPbK7jB7FEJjtK9foAvesOifac-6bSljI6uPlmSqSHBIRxZ46siFlJGVmf3tZ6feChfOL-jKjx_kaZvaAWTqg.bqPhXDl7sl1Y16GkEl8OK5-4L5M' in template to unicode. return concat(self.root_render_func(self.new_context(vars))) ' in template to unicode. return concat(self.root_render_func(self.new_context(vars))) /home/jmartinm/.virtualenvs/pu-inspire2/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/jinja2/environment.py:966: RuntimeWarning: Convert string '/home/jmartinm/.virtualenvs/pu-inspire2/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/jinja2/environment.py:966: RuntimeWarning: Convert string '0.00059700012207' in template to unicode. return concat(self.root_render_func(self.new_context(vars))) ' in template to unicode. return concat(self.root_render_func(self.new_context(vars))) /home/jmartinm/.virtualenvs/pu-inspire2/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/jinja2/environment.py:966: RuntimeWarning: Convert string '/home/jmartinm/.virtualenvs/pu-inspire2/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/flask_debugtoolbar/templates/panels/sqlalchemy.html:31: RuntimeWarning: Convert string '
SELECT format.id AS format_id, format.name AS format_name, format.code AS format_code, format.description AS format_description, format.content_type AS format_content_type, format.mime_type AS format_mime_type, format.visibility AS format_visibility, format.last_updated AS format_last_updated 
FROM format 
WHERE format.content_type != %s AND format.visibility = %s ORDER BY format.name
' in template to unicode. {% trans "Line" %} ' in template to unicode. return concat(self.root_render_func(self.new_context(vars))) ``` I understand the unicode issue in the templates is worth fixing, but it: 1. Makes it difficult to work with, as all those messages are mixed up with messages you might be interested it while working on other features. 2. Don't seem helpful, as I don't see any pointer to the template that has the problem and what exactly the problem is. (well i know it is a unicode issue, but not exactly what to do about it) So, can we either hide those messages or make them more explicit so that people that see them can actually fix the problem? I don't think in the current state they are going to have the desired effect.

issue_title: MySQL warning is spamming logs
issue_body:
We realize that in several case with recent enough mysql instances, logs were spammed by this type of WARNING:

WARNING: <class '_mysql_exceptions.Warning'>: 'INSERT DELAYED' is deprecated and will be removed in a future release. Please use INSERT instead (/usr/lib64/p
ython2.7/site-packages/invenio/dbquery.py:258)

It would be really useful if a way to solve it was implemented.

issue_title: search: change license (simple case)
views: SSE integration
issue_body:
Add the ability to publish SSE events, which correspond to a particular deposit.

One possible solution is to register an endpoint to each deposit (i.e. `/deposits/<depid>/sse`), where a GET request would return an established connection with an SSE channel (having the same name as the deposit URL). Therefore, all handling of access rights concerning the SSE subscriptions will be provided by the `read_permission_factory` on the deposit.

issue_body:
**Scenario:**
- call `invenio records delete <ID>`
- call `invenio records delete --force <ID>`

=> this fails

**Problem:**
`invenio records delete` [calls `get_records` without setting `with_deleted=True`](https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio-records/blob/c4af2bd5b67ae6bb627d01cd97d58b0417baf810/invenio_records/cli.py#L166).

runzeo error (Plone 5/Python 2.7): Attempted relative import in non-package
issue_body:
In order to not run into the problems of #84 I tried to use ``runzeo`` with supervisor. 

``runzeo`` fails to start with:

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/home/jensens/.buildout/shared-eggs/ZEO-5.1.0-py2.7.egg/ZEO/runzeo.py", line 401, in <module>
    main()
  File "/home/jensens/.buildout/shared-eggs/ZEO-5.1.0-py2.7.egg/ZEO/runzeo.py", line 392, in main
    s.main()
  File "/home/jensens/.buildout/shared-eggs/ZEO-5.1.0-py2.7.egg/ZEO/runzeo.py", line 155, in main
    self.create_server()
  File "/home/jensens/.buildout/shared-eggs/ZEO-5.1.0-py2.7.egg/ZEO/runzeo.py", line 251, in create_server
    self.server = create_server(self.storages, self.options)
  File "/home/jensens/.buildout/shared-eggs/ZEO-5.1.0-py2.7.egg/ZEO/runzeo.py", line 345, in create_server
    from .StorageServer import StorageServer
ValueError: Attempted relative import in non-package
```

I added a configuration to the Plone coredev buildout here:
https://github.com/plone/buildout.coredev/blob/5.1/experimental/zeoserver.cfg
and run it with ./bin/buildout -c experimental/zeoserver.cfg
The generated script ``./bin/zeoserver`` has two modes, one uses ``zeoctl`` and the other, if in enviroment ``SUPERVISOR_ENABLED=1`` is set, ``runzeo`` is used.

After changing the relative import to an absolute one this specific problem is gone and ``runzeo`` or ``SUPERVISOR_ENABLED=1 ./bin/zeoserver`` works (but #84 is still an issue).

issue_title: lxml uses etree.so that throws an unexpected e_machine error
issue_body:
If I use lxml in my app the app crashes with this error:
logcat:

> **ImportError: dlopen failed: "/data/data/org.apptest.test/files/app/lib/python2.7/site-packages/lxml/etree.so" has unexpected e_machine: 3**

I tried in a 64bit and 32bit debian installation, message changes, so I suspect that etree.so is compiled with the hosting machine on which buildozer runs instead of the target android machine.


in  64 bit enviroment I get:

> **ImportError: dlopen failed: "/data/data/org.apptest.test/files/app/lib/python2.7/site-packages/lxml/etree.so" is 64-bit instead of 32-bit**

issue_title: Non-deterministic calculating issue
issue_body:
I don't know why this is happening but sometimes my evaluate gives 0.0 for the output of something that should be about 1.0 about 92 times in 3000 executions. I would love to get the right answer every time, if possible. My test code is

    from numexpr import evaluate
    import numpy as np
    
    failure = 0
    for n in range(3000):
        x = np.ones((1500, 1500), dtype=np.complex128)
        X = evaluate('x.real * x.real + x.imag * x.imag', local_dict={'x': x})
        if X[0, 0] < 0.5:
            failure += 1
    
    print('numexpr failed ' + str(failure) + ' times out of 3000')

My platform is Anaconda python=3.4 for Win64. The relevant package versions are
numexpr: 2.4.4-np19py34_p0 [mkl]
mkl:     11.1-np19py34_p1
numpy:   1.9.3-py34_p0     [mkl]

I have run this code on two different intel processors from two years ago and more like 7 years ago and I get the same behavior. The number of failures and their order are never the same.

issue_title: Better timeout message in CallbackHandler
issue_body: Right now the `CallbackHandler`'s timeout msg only includes the `callback_id`, which is an implementation detail that doesn't make too much sense for the user. We should add the name of the call where the event was supposed to originate from.

issue_body: Travis couldn't support windows runs. So we need to figure out how to run presubmit unit tests on windows

issue_title: [Feature request] More breadcrumbs configuration options
issue_body:
Hi,

We just updated raven and sentry and we were very happy with the "new" breadcrumbs feature.
However, our excitation faded away, when we found out that each new event on sentry intead of taking ~0,5kb, now takes ~70kb. This means that our HDD usage would rise 100x from what we have now.
When dealing with Really Big Crashes (resulting in hundreds of events per second), 100-fold rise in HTTP transfer is 100 times more likely to hang both the application and sentry. That's the price we cannot afford to pay for some extra information provided by breadrumbs.

In fact, we would love to use Breadcrumbs, but on a much smaller scale, say 10 breadrumbs.

For this we need the option to set the size of breadcrumbs buffer lower than the default limit 100. However, currently there is no way of providing this via any setting, argument or keyword argument - see https://github.com/getsentry/raven-python/blob/master/raven/breadcrumbs.py#L76

On the other hand, raven-js supports this out of the box via maxBreadrumbs setting ( https://docs.sentry.io/clients/javascript/config/ ). It would be very nice to introduce this to raven-python as well :)

issue_title: Error parsing simple DESCRIBE query
issue_body:
A simple query of the form `DESCRIBE <http://example.org/>` results in the following stacktrace.

```
  File "/Users/simonjo/anaconda/lib/python3.5/site-packages/rdflib/plugins/sparql/processor.py", line 75, in query
    query = translateQuery(parsetree, base, initNs)
  File "/Users/simonjo/anaconda/lib/python3.5/site-packages/rdflib/plugins/sparql/algebra.py", line 766, in translateQuery
    P, PV = translate(q[1])
  File "/Users/simonjo/anaconda/lib/python3.5/site-packages/rdflib/plugins/sparql/algebra.py", line 549, in translate
    M = translateGroupGraphPattern(q.where)
  File "/Users/simonjo/anaconda/lib/python3.5/site-packages/rdflib/plugins/sparql/algebra.py", line 269, in translateGroupGraphPattern
    if graphPattern.name == 'SubSelect':
AttributeError: 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'name'
```

The call to `translateGroupGraphPattern` on line 549 in `algebra.py` states incorrectly that queries always include a where clause. This is not the case for DESCRIBE, see SPARQL 1.1 section 16.4.1 (SPARQL 1.0 section 10.4.1) "Explicit IRIs".

```
    # all query types have a where part
    M = translateGroupGraphPattern(q.where)
```

issue_title: Analytic Zernike gradients
issue_body:
During the DESC hack week, I implemented photon shooting for optical PSFs by computing finite difference gradients of optical phase screens.  

It would probably be faster and more accurate to use an analytic expression for the gradients of Zernike polynomials, however.  

[This paper](https://www.osapublishing.org/josaa/abstract.cfm?uri=josaa-31-4-708) shows how to express circular Zernike gradients as Zernike polynomials with different coefficients, though it may also be possible to compute the derivatives directly from OpticalScreen.coef_array, which would then apply to both circular and annular Zernikes.

issue_title: Partial execution provenance storage
issue_body: Currently, all the provenance is stored at the end of the execution. It is necessary to create a function that partially stores it to allow users to use it for debugging (#58) and monitoring (#64)

issue_title: Work with ids during execution collection
issue_body:
Currently, noWorkflow uses a "complex" data structure during execution: the Activation object has a list of children activations, a list of Object Values (for arguments, and globals), a list of File Access.

When we save it, we have to navigate the whole activation stack in order to set the actual id and save the activations in the correct order. 

It makes it harder to add features such as partial saving the data, since it has a very specific order  to save.

We could use something simpler that calculates its __id__ during the execution and just store it as is.

In this issue, I propose to stop using a global __id__ as primary key of Activation, ObjectValue, FileAccess and use restart the __id__ for every trial and use the combination __trial_id__, __id__ 

It is already implemented at the ObjectStore class for Program Slicing variables, dependencies and usages, so it won't be hard to adapt to Profiler.

However, this change will break all the existing databases, and may break other parts of the code.

issue_title: Add note to readme.md: to build with bundle, less@1.7.5 or lower is required
issue_body:
A minor change to the readme.md file would be nice, since this confused me a bit when I tried to build the project using `bundle exec jekyll build`. The following error was shown (included here for completeness):

```
Configuration file: /Users/msikma/Source/Font-Awesome/_config.yml
            Source: src
       Destination: _gh_pages
      Generating... Compiling Less files
lessc assets/font-awesome/less/font-awesome.less > assets/font-awesome/css/font-awesome.css
lessc --compress assets/font-awesome/less/font-awesome.less > assets/font-awesome/css/font-awesome.min.css
lessc --yui-compress assets/less/site.less > assets/css/site.css
make: *** [build] Error 1
done.
```

The problem is that the `--yui-compress` argument is not supported as of less 2.0.0. So in order to successfully compile the project, the user needs to first do some thing like `npm install -g less@1.7.5` (latest pre-2.0.0 version).

It could also be added to the project's dependencies. Alternatively, the build process could be rewritten to work properly with whatever ended up replacing that argument.

issue_title: Add option for terse logging.
issue_body:
When using pip-accel for e.g. Travis-CI, the log output is much to long:
```
2015-10-19 23:50:55 testing-worker-linux-docker-357adccc-3199-linux-7 pip.req.req_set[3303] INFO Requirement already satisfied (use --upgrade to upgrade): wheel in /home/travis/virtualenv/python2.7.9/lib/python2.7/site-packages (from -r tests/requirements.txt (line 9))
2015-10-19 23:50:55 testing-worker-linux-docker-357adccc-3199-linux-7 pip.download[3303] INFO File was already downloaded /home/travis/.cache/pip-accel/sources/check_manifest-0.25-py2.py3-none-any.whl
```

Neither timestamp nor hostname, not even the process-id is meaning full here. Instead aligning the length of the logger names would ease reading the logs.

Please add an option for setting the log format, or at least for disabling timestamp and hostname. Another option would be to add an option to use the standard logging module instead of colorlogs.

(When thinking about this, the later option would a) quickly be implemented, b) allow configuring the logformat later c) reuse the pip-accel config-file for defining the loggers (see https://docs.python.org/2/howto/logging.html#configuring-logging)

issue_title: Sometimes Patroni fails to drop replication slot and it causes xlog to fill up
issue_body:
We already observed it several times on production.
I think it happens because we are removing only non-active replication slots: https://github.com/zalando/patroni/blob/master/patroni/postgresql.py#L1184

Default value of [wal_sender_timeout](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/9.6/static/runtime-config-replication.html) is 60 seconds while we are trying to remove replication slot after 30 seconds (ttl=30). That means postgres might think that slot is still active and does nothing. Patroni at the same time thinks that it was removed successfully.

issue_title: Question: setting CMAKE_BUILD_TYPE
issue_body:
This is a question about style, rather than about right or wrong

What we do sometimes in our packages is to provide a default build type: something along the lines of 
```cmake
if(NOT CMAKE_BUILD_TYPE)
  set(CMAKE_BUILD_TYPE Release)
endif()
```
This still allows for setting the build type from the command line and has been discussed in various threads on the CMake Mailing Lists and in other places as a viable solution. Also, it is not forbidden by the CMake Coding Standard on the catkin docs (http://docs.ros.org/indigo/api/catkin/html/user_guide/standards.html).
Granted, this is merely for ease-of-use and might be error-prone, if not done carefully.

catkin_lint reports this as an error and thus stops the build, if integrated in the build process.

What about setting this to a warning? Or do you feel the above is bad style and should not under any circumstances be done?

(Love this tool, btw. Thanks!)

issue_title: RHEL/CentOS 7 support on PyPI
issue_body:
I need this library on CentOS 7 using Python 3 from http://ius.io/ . As mentioned in #5, the current package on PyPI, i.e. the recent `systemd-python==232`, doesn't work on CentOS 7. `from systemd import journal` fails with `undefined symbol: sd_journal_open_files_fd`. (On a side note, I'm a bit confused as this project's README states that it's already packaged for Python3 in the official RHEL/CentOS repos, but I can't find any sign of that, or even Python 3 itself).

As RHEL/CentOS are major distributions and represent a big chunk of people, I'd like to request that a compatible version of systemd-python be pushed to PyPI. Perhaps the support for `sd_journal_open_files_fd` can be made backward-compatible. Or maybe it would be necessary to push up an older build.

I also wouldn't particularly mind building the package myself, but I haven't had any luck getting it to work, so some guidance would be welcome (after installing, the above import causes `ImportError: No module named 'systemd._journal'`). Thus far, my searches have been fruitless.

I'm not sure if this is accurate, but either way, if we're following the convention of datastore/__init__.py, this should be:

>>> from gcloud import datastore

```

issue_title: Subscribe experimental doesn't appear to be retrying on retryable exceptions, also doesn't surface the error.
issue_labels: [{'id': 139685153, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWwxMzk2ODUxNTM=', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/labels/api:%20pubsub', 'name': 'api: pubsub', 'color': 'f8f8f8', 'default': False}, {'id': 544604244, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWw1NDQ2MDQyNDQ=', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/labels/priority:%20p1', 'name': 'priority: p1', 'color': 'ffa03e', 'default': False}, {'id': 486182624, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWw0ODYxODI2MjQ=', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/labels/release%20blocking', 'name': 'release blocking', 'color': 'ffa03e', 'default': False}, {'id': 77438580, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWw3NzQzODU4MA==', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/labels/type:%20bug', 'name': 'type: bug', 'color': 'db4437', 'default': False}]
issue_body:
Full context at https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/issues/4841#issuecomment-386988007 downwards.

gist with logs: https://gist.github.com/psalaberria002/e91956d94e82d706d65da7111a40ca24

Possible culprits:

1. `send()` and `recv()` expectedly error on `next(self.call)`, but if that was a *retryable* error that somehow re-bubbled, it would not trigger any callbacks.
2. `bidi` uses `rpc._wrapped` because `api_core` doesn't appropriately expose `add_done_callback`. This means that bidi unwraps all of the retry logic.

This doesn't explain *why* retrying a perfectly normal looking error somehow still leads us to a broken stream, unless the newly opened stream also has an error. But it's unclear why that wouldn't happen when calling the initial RPC.

6. Steps to reproduce: Configure a custom Python log level with `logging.addLevelName` and configure `google-cloud-logging`.
7. Code example

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python

"""
log.py

Custom standard library compliant logging implementation.

Add audit logging level to the standard library logging module.
The configure method is called by Django configuration hooks,
and it is configured with the LOGGING_CONFIG Django setting value.

Courtesy of Thomas Fauskanger
- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/47888855/python3-add-logging-level
- https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/2.0/ref/settings/#std:setting-LOGGING_CONFIG
- https://docs.python.org/3/library/logging.html#logging.setLoggerClass
- https://docs.python.org/3/library/logging.html#logging.addLevelName
"""

import os
import logging.config
import google.cloud.logging


class AuditLogger(logging.Logger):
    NAME = 'AUDIT'
    LEVEL = logging.CRITICAL + 10

    def audit(self, message, *args, **kwargs):
        if self.isEnabledFor(self.LEVEL):
            self._log(self.LEVEL, message, args, **kwargs)


def configure(config=None):
    logging.setLoggerClass(AuditLogger)
    logging.addLevelName(AuditLogger.LEVEL, AuditLogger.NAME)
    logging.config.dictConfig(config or {'version': 1, 'disable_existing_loggers': False})

    client = google.cloud.logging.Client()
    client.setup_logging()


def test():
    logging.getLogger(__name__).audit('This will crash GCP logging')


if __name__ == '__main__':
    os.environ.setdefault('GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS', 'tmp/gcplogging/gcp.json')
    configure()
    test()
```

8. Suggested fix: Support users registering their own custom log levels and mapping them into Stackdriver Logging internal logging level in addition to using the current static protobuf logging levels. This could be done by offering an API similar to `logging.addLevelName` with e.g. extra argument for registering the Stackdriver Logging equivalent log level name. In our case it would be sensible to map the log level to something like `NOTICE` or higher. This could be achieved with a simple dictionary name mapping and lookup on the Python end, resulting in very low overheads.

We could then iterate to something like:

```python
import logging
import google.cloud.logging

logging.setLoggerClass(AuditLogger)
logging.addLevelName(AuditLogger.LEVEL, AuditLogger.NAME)
logging.config.dictConfig(config or {'version': 1, 'disable_existing_loggers': False})

client = google.cloud.logging.Client()
# the following is just a demonstration of an idea; probably not the right call target
client.addLevelName(AuditLogger.LEVEL, AuditLogger.NAME, 'NOTICE')  
client.setup_logging()
```

issue_body:

2. Python version and virtual environment information `python --version`

--> 404         rows_data = _rows_from_json(response.get('rows', ()), self.schema)

/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/google/cloud/bigquery/_helpers.pyc in _rows_from_json(rows, schema)

    103     for field, cell in zip(schema, row['f']):

    106             row_data.append([converter(item, field)

KeyError: u'DATETIME'
```
5. Steps to reproduce

issue_title: Is MergeFrom used by accident (instead of CopyFrom) in bigtable.Table.create?
issue_body:
See [source][1]. As an example of why `MergeFrom` instead of `CopyFrom` can be a bug:

```python
>>> from google.protobuf.timestamp_pb2 import Timestamp
>>> from google.cloud.bigtable._generated.bigtable_instance_admin_pb2 import (
...     CreateInstanceMetadata)
>>>
>>> metadata1 = CreateInstanceMetadata(request_time=Timestamp(nanos=100))
>>> metadata2 = CreateInstanceMetadata(request_time=Timestamp(nanos=100))
>>> t = Timestamp(seconds=1000)
>>> metadata1
request_time {
  nanos: 100
}

>>> metadata2
request_time {
  nanos: 100
}

>>> t
seconds: 1000

>>>
>>> metadata1.request_time.MergeFrom(t)
>>> metadata2.request_time.CopyFrom(t)
>>>
>>> metadata1
request_time {
  seconds: 1000
  nanos: 100
}

>>> metadata2
request_time {
  seconds: 1000
}
```

[1]: https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/blob/2d81f88454724f0a672b453c994413c3cf1110c7/bigtable/google/cloud/bigtable/table.py#L184

issue_title: inverted ceil() and floor() in make-row mixin
issue_body:
I just came across a minor issue while debugging another.

I think ceil() and floor() are inverted in the make-row mixin here.
https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap-sass/blob/master/assets/stylesheets/bootstrap/mixins/_grid.scss#L16

The rounded number will always be negative so the result will actually work the opposite way as it does in the col mixins here:
https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap-sass/blob/master/assets/stylesheets/bootstrap/mixins/_grid-framework.scss#L16

In a nutshell the bug shifts the visible content 1px to the right if the grid-gutter-width is an odd number.

little example (first row broken, second default, third fixed):
https://codepen.io/quarkus/pen/oGydpW?editors=1100

Pretty hard to see as its just one pixel, but its noticable on the left edge of the cols

issue_title: Overlaying Config Files is broken
issue_body:
http://www.pantsbuild.org/options.html#overlaying-config-files documents that one can do:

    $ ./pants --pants-config-files=a.ini --pants-config-files=b.ini options --options-name="level"
    level = info (from CONFIG in a.ini)

    $ cat a.ini
    [GLOBAL]
    level: info

    $ cat b.ini
    [GLOBAL]
    level: debug

According to the docs, the second --pants-config-files should overlay the earlier values, but this is not happening :/

issue_title: `IsortPythonTask` should use the invalidation and caching subsystems
issue_body: Right now it gets the job done, but always does the work, necessitating hacks like this pants pipeline: https://rbcommons.com/s/twitter/r/4289/

issue_body:
Right now in order to install a plugin sdist you need to:

1. create a venv bootstrap script
2. add the plugin requirement to the venv
3. discover the backend register module package and add that to pants.ini DEFAULT.backend_packages.

Items 1 & 2 can be addressed by https://github.com/pantsbuild/setup/issues/3 but item 3 can be eliminated by using the already existing `DEFAULT.plugins` option and handling.  In order for https://github.com/pantsbuild/setup/issues/3 to leverage `DEFAULT.plugins` though, pants needs to start publishing its plugins with proper entry_points defined.

issue_title: Building Play Apps
issue_body:
We have a large codebase with lots of scala/python, and also use [Play](https://www.playframework.com/) for our webapps. We'd love to use Pants, but building the Play app is a blocker.

From a cursory scan at very least, Pants would need to:
* compile routes file (yaml) into scala
* compile html templates into scala
* package javascript/css assets. This includes its whole own pipeline (webpack, minify, etc)
* build all those parts together
* add in play's runner (the main entrypoint)

issue_title: Add a partially-multi-threading Engine subclass
issue_body:
The existing `exp.Engine` subclasses are "all or nothing", in that they execute all tasks in another process, or no tasks in another process.

In the case of many `exp.nodes.Node` implementations, switching processes is overkill... but switching threads would probably be worthwhile for a few of them:
1. FilesystemNode - python releases the GIL during io-related syscalls, so multithreading for filesystem operations may result in an overall speedup
2. (planned) ProcessNode - a forked process is definitely worth babysitting using a thread; see #2978

The proposal is to add an Engine subclass that uses filters to decide which Nodes to execute in threads, and which to run inline.

issue_title: Update troubleshooting doc to include info about debugging buildcache issues
issue_body: http://pantsbuild.github.io/tshoot.html doesn't talk at all about buildcache. There are circumstances when you have a corrupt entry and you need to work around it. It's tricky because you have to both remove the local buildcache cache and run `./pants clean-all` in the same action or the corrupt entry will not go away.

issue_title: Exporting to CSV after python parse
issue_body:
First of all - Love this tremendously. Second, I've been trying to use the usaddress.py + csv.py + pandas to import from a csv, parse the rows, then export the rows back out into a csv. 

However, when I run the code listed below, it (1) doesn't parse the column headers correctly and (2) doesn't then parse the fields correctly. This approached was recommended to me [over on StackOverflow] (https://stackoverflow.com/questions/31798061/output-a-changing-ordereddict-to-csv), so please let me know if (1) This approach is silly and/or (2) I'm merely suffering from noob-itis as I am, in fact, terribly new to python.

    import pandas as pd
    import usaddress
    from pandas import DataFrame

    data = ['Robie House, 5757 South Woodlawn Avenue, Chicago, IL 60637',
        'State & Lake, Chicago']

    tagged_addresses = [usaddress.parse(line) for line in data]

    address_df = pd.DataFrame(tagged_addresses)

    address_df.to_csv('moooutput.csv')

issue_title: Verify training data in f731af
issue_body:
I added training data from `unparseable.csv` before I had a solid understanding of the existing training data and the URISA standard. I'm wondering if this is contributing to depreciating performance in #153 and #155.

I'm going to look through the new training data in [f731af](https://github.com/datamade/usaddress/commit/f731afdb58f32c47c0fea9ba2f79ebea4c26ad63) and verify that it 1) meets the URISA standard and 2) does not include excessive examples of unusual address patterns.

issue_title: add loading indicators to all content renderers
issue_labels: [{'id': 492821376, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/learningequality/kolibri/labels/DEV:%20frontend', 'name': 'DEV: frontend', 'color': 'DFDFDF', 'default': False}, {'id': 492818262, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/learningequality/kolibri/labels/TASK:%20new%20feature', 'name': 'TASK: new feature', 'color': '72486f', 'default': False}, {'id': 492816719, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/learningequality/kolibri/labels/TASK:%20ux%20update', 'name': 'TASK: ux update', 'color': '72486f', 'default': False}, {'id': 831979218, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/learningequality/kolibri/labels/changelog', 'name': 'changelog', 'color': '000000', 'default': False}]
issue_body:
### Observed behavior

In many situations it will take some time for content to be rendered, and during that time the user is left confused.

### Expected behavior

All content renderers should show a loading indicator until the content is done loading

### User-facing consequences

Confusion, and potentially navigating away before content is done loading


------

cc @christianmemije

issue_title: re-introduce <script> blocks to code examples
issue_body:
In a44a4fc1523eb468fc515644f5e6e5ad83ee4117 we removed empty `<script>` blocks because they seem pointless. Unfortunately, this confuses `vuep`, which helpfully tries to add them back in, without success:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/2367265/29235759-de7ecf14-7eb6-11e7-9949-ca39c73a48c8.png)

A compromise is to use an empty `<script></script>` block:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/2367265/29235767-04b755e8-7eb7-11e7-90a5-d9121be84927.png)

issue_title: Support overriding the aws_config_dir via environmental variable
issue_body: It would be great if the aws_config_dir variable could be overridden by an environmental variable. The awscli library supports reading from the AWS_CONFIG_FILE environmental variable for setups where the config file may not live in ~/.aws/config.

issue_title: PyMISP performance issue
issue_body:
**Work environment** 

Questions | Answers
-- | --
Type of issue | Potential Bug?
OS version (server) | Red Hat Enterprise Linux Server release 7.4 
OS version (client) | Windows 10
PHP version | 5.6.25
MISP version / git hash | v2.4.88 / db02fcc4d466590bec47c587102ab66b386d8545
PyMISP version | 2.4.87.1
Browser | N/A

**Concern**

We have been creating a script to make calls to MISP, and noticed that it seems requests via the PyMISP library is can be ~50% slower or more then making pure requests to MISP. Could this be linked to a bug?

For a search that looks for events in the last 5 hours, this the discrepancy that we saw.

_PyMISP time: 0:01:22.506460_
`results_pymisp = PyMISP(misp_url, misp_api_key, False, 'json').search(controller='attributes', type_attribute=types, category=cat, last=time_period, enforceWarninglist=True)`

_Requests time: 0:00:09.762386_
```
url_pull = '{}attributes/restSearch/json'.format(misp_url)
headers_pull = {'Authorization': misp_api_key, 'Content-Type': 'application/json',
                'Accept': 'application/json'}
data_pull = {"request": {"type": types, "category": cat, "last": time_period,
                         "enforceWarnlinglist": "True"}}
requests_response = requests.post(url_pull, headers=headers_pull, json=data_pull, verify=False)
```

I also tired this on a local VM image running on Ubuntu with MISP_v2.4.88, and it seemed a similar issue was occurring.

issue_title: "Unrecognized arguments" in CLI Example
issue_body:
From the example in the 'confidant help' command, this: 

`confidant get_service -u "https://confidant-production.example.com" -k "alias/authnz-production" --from myservice-production --to confidant-production --user_type service --region us-west-2 --service myservice-production`

yields the error

`confidant: error: unrecognized arguments: -u https://confidant-production.example.com -k alias/authnz-production --from myservice-production --to confidant-production --user_type service --region us-west-2`

It should yield a url unreachable or kms auth error, not an argument error if the example is correct.

issue_title: Resource IDs are not checked
issue_body: This module does not check that the IDs of resources are integers. That can lead to SQL injection attempts being sent to the Mangopay API.

issue_title: potential for [Errno 18] Invalid cross-device link

if atomic file write (via rename) occurs across devices. For example, if /tmp directory is mounted on a different device

the implementation should write the tmp file to the same directory as the destination

issue_title: Pulling an image for run much slower than commandline
issue_body:
Running the same command on an image that hasn't been downloaded is much slower with the SDK than with the CLI. Does the CLI have access to some other cache than the SDK? Are the two tests not really comparable? I'm new with this, so apologies if I'm not using it correctly.

### Context:
```
$ pip freeze | grep docker && python --version && docker version
docker==2.1.0
docker-pycreds==0.2.1
Python 2.7.13 :: Continuum Analytics, Inc.
Client:
 Version:      17.03.0-ce
 API version:  1.26
 Go version:   go1.7.5
 Git commit:   60ccb22
 Built:        Thu Feb 23 10:40:59 2017
 OS/Arch:      darwin/amd64

Server:
 Version:      17.03.0-ce
 API version:  1.26 (minimum version 1.12)
 Go version:   go1.7.5
 Git commit:   3a232c8
 Built:        Tue Feb 28 07:52:04 2017
 OS/Arch:      linux/amd64
 Experimental: true
$ sw_vers -productVersion
10.11.6 # MacOS
```

### Reproducer:
```python
import docker
import unittest
from subprocess import Popen, PIPE

class DockerTests(unittest.TestCase):
    def test_hello_world_sdk(self):
        # Slow if image not already downloaded
        client = docker.from_env()
        output = client.containers.run("ubuntu", "echo hello world")
        self.assertEqual(output, 'hello world\n')

    def test_hello_world_popen(self):
        # Even if download is necessary, runs in a couple seconds
        p = Popen(['docker', 'run', 'ubuntu', 'echo', 'hello', 'world'],
                  stdout=PIPE)
        output = p.stdout.read()
        self.assertEqual(output, 'hello world\n')
```
Using the SDK is fast if the image has already been downloaded:
```
$ python -m unittest -v docker_engine_app.tests.DockerTests.test_hello_world_sdk
test_hello_world_sdk (docker_engine_app.tests.DockerTests) ... ok

----------------------------------------------------------------------
Ran 1 test in 1.557s

OK
```
But much slower starting from scratch:
```
$ docker rmi ubuntu
Untagged: ubuntu:latest
$ python -m unittest -v docker_engine_app.tests.DockerTests.test_hello_world_sdk
test_hello_world_sdk (docker_engine_app.tests.DockerTests) ... ok

----------------------------------------------------------------------
Ran 1 test in 57.183s

OK
```
But if the CLI needs to download the image, it isn't anywhere near as slow.
```
$ docker rmi ubuntu
Untagged: ubuntu:latest
$ python -m unittest -v docker_engine_app.tests.DockerTests.test_hello_world_popen
test_hello_world_popen (docker_engine_app.tests.DockerTests) ... Unable to find image 'ubuntu:latest' locally
latest: Pulling from library/ubuntu
Digest: sha256:dd7808d8792c9841d0b460122f1acf0a2dd1f56404f8d1e56298048885e45535
Status: Downloaded newer image for ubuntu:latest
ok

----------------------------------------------------------------------
Ran 1 test in 2.298s

OK
```

issue_title: Bug in VariableElimination
issue_body:
```python
cpd_accident = TabularCPD('accident', 2, [[0.2], [0.8]])

cpd_traffic_jam = TabularCPD('traffic_jam', 2, [[0.9, 0.6, 0.7, 0.1],[0.1, 0.4, 0.3, 0.9]], evidence=['rain', 'accident'], evidence_card=[2, 2])

cpd_long_queues = TabularCPD('long_queues', 2, [[0.9, 0.2], [0.1, 0.8]], evidence=['traffic_jam'], evidence_card=[2])

cpd_getting_up_late = TabularCPD('getting_up_late', 2, [[0.6], [0.4]])

cpd_late_for_school = TabularCPD('late_for_school', 2, [[0.9, 0.45, 0.8, 0.1], [0.1, 0.55, 0.2, 0.9]], evidence=['getting_up_late', 'traffic_jam'], evidence_card=[2, 2])

cpd_rain = TabularCPD('rain', 2, [[0.4], [0.6]])

model = BayesianModel([('accident', 'traffic_jam'), ('rain', 'traffic_jam'), ('traffic_jam', 'late_for_school'), ('traffic_jam', 'long_queues'), ('getting_up_late', 'late_for_school')])

model.add_cpds(cpd_accident, cpd_traffic_jam, cpd_long_queues, cpd_getting_up_late, cpd_late_for_school, cpd_rain)

from pgmpy.inference import VariableElimination

infer = VariableElimination(model)

infer.query(['rain'], evidence={'traffic_jam': 1})

Traceback (most recent call last):

  File "<ipython-input-29-75d9f03ac72c>", line 1, in <module>
    infer.query(['rain'], evidence={'traffic_jam': 1})

  File "/home/ankur/pgmpy/pgmpy/inference/ExactInference.py", line 120, in query
    evidence=evidence, elimination_order=elimination_order)

  File "/home/ankur/pgmpy/pgmpy/inference/ExactInference.py", line 49, in _variable_elimination
    inplace=False)

  File "/home/ankur/pgmpy/pgmpy/factors/Factor.py", line 274, in reduce
    value_indices = list(map(int, value_row[1]))

ValueError: invalid literal for int() with base 10: 'jam'
```
Happening because of our internal representation of variable with states (var_state) and since the variable name here is `traffic_jam`, it's splitting it from here.

https://github.com/pgmpy/pgmpy/issues/392 should fix this also.

issue_title: Insecure allowedOrigins validation
issue_body:
Autobahn|Python incorrectly checks the Origin header when the 'allowedOrigins' value is set.

The following will set
```
class OriginCheckServerFactory(WebSocketServerFactory):
    protocol = ...arbitrary entry here...

    def __init__(self, url):
        WebSocketServerFactory.__init__(self, url)
        self.setProtocolOptions(allowedOrigins=[u"127.0.0.1",u"*.example.com"])
```

Then the following connection request will result in a valid 101 Protocol Switch Response:

```
GET /ws HTTP/1.1
Host: www.example.com
Sec-WebSocket-Version: 13
Origin: http://www.example.com.malicious.com
Sec-WebSocket-Extensions: permessage-deflate
Sec-WebSocket-Key: tXAxWFUqnhi86Ajj7dRY5g==
Connection: keep-alive, Upgrade
Upgrade: websocket
```

This is due to the [wildcard2patterns](https://github.com/crossbario/autobahn-python/blob/master/autobahn/util.py#L632) functions, which turns `u"*.example.com"` into `r".*\.example\.com"`. This regex pattern is then [matched against the complete incoming origin value](https://github.com/crossbario/autobahn-python/blob/1c86fefa733901247d6439af346d77cbb58a9bf4/autobahn/websocket/protocol.py#L2588). The websocket_origin value should be first parsed as a URI object and the authority section extracted, and the regex matched against authority ensuring that ^ and $ are used. E.g. `r"^.*\.example\.com$"`
