CoAP Request URI:
NON POST coap://[<peer address>]:MM/a/ae

Transaction Pattern:
Ntf/Qry/Ans_CON

issue_title: api: simplify OT api by removing any locking/concurrency support.
issue_body:
Currently, a subset of OT calls are designed to be called from interrupt context when another call to OT may be interrupted.  It is preferable for all locking/concurrency handling to be handled outside of OT, as this is necessarily platform-specific.  This would also greatly simplify the `otPlat*` driver APIs.

Remove any locking/concurrency handling from OT would mean:

1. Removing the Atomic platform abstraction and any use of Atomic within OT.
2. Removing all `otPlat*Signal*` calls into OT.
3. Updating the POSIX example to use a single thread of execution.

            for i in range(f_shape[2]):
                weights[:, :, i, i] = bilinear
```
f_shape[2] and f_shape[3] may not be the same size and as a result, the weights are improperly initialized.

I propose:

```
            for i in range(f_shape[2]):
                for j in range(f_shape[3]):
                    weights[:, :, i, j] = bilinear

```

If this makes sense, I will make a pull request for it.

issue_title: Handcrafting guide is broken while using json dumps to serialize handcrafted tx to json str
issue_body:
* bigchaindb-driver version: bigchaindb-driver==0.4.0
* Python version: Python 3.5.2
* Operating System: Ubuntu 16.04.2 LTS(Xenial)

### Description
While following the handcrafting guide for a CREATE transaction, came across an issue after creating a transaction ID right after the **Up to now** section. I was trying to serialize the **handcrafted_creation_tx** to json string as per the guide and it fails by complaining that the transaction ID is not JSON serializable. Same scenario with **prepared_creation_tx**

### What I Did

```
json_str_handcrafted_tx = json.dumps(handcrafted_creation_tx, sort_keys=True)
AND
json_str_prepared_tx = json.dumps(prepared_creation_tx, sort_keys=True)
```
###### Traceback
```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/usr/lib/python3.5/json/__init__.py", line 237, in dumps
    **kw).encode(obj)
  File "/usr/lib/python3.5/json/encoder.py", line 198, in encode
    chunks = self.iterencode(o, _one_shot=True)
  File "/usr/lib/python3.5/json/encoder.py", line 256, in iterencode
    return _iterencode(o, 0)
  File "/usr/lib/python3.5/json/encoder.py", line 179, in default
    raise TypeError(repr(o) + " is not JSON serializable")
TypeError: b'be9cd8825d6d7cf5f1ac81883e9c6b7c43f7ada92a4321c135c5ec5a2f9c637b' is not JSON serializable
```

###### Solution

1. One fix can be to use rapidjson for the aforementioned commands:
```
import rapidjson
json_str_handcrafted_tx = rapidjson.dumps(handcrafted_creation_tx, skipkeys=False, ensure_ascii=False, sort_keys=True)
json_str_prepared_tx = rapidjson.dumps(prepared_creation_tx, skipkeys=False, ensure_ascii=False, sort_keys=True)
```
2. Another fix can be to decode the 'id' to utf-8 before calling json.dumps
```
handcrafted_creation_tx['id'] = handcrafted_creation_tx['id'].decode('utf-8')
prepared_creation_tx['id'] = prepared_creation_tx['id'].decode('utf-8')

json_str_handcrafted_tx = json.dumps(handcrafted_creation_tx, sort_keys=True)
json_str_prepared_tx = json.dumps(prepared_creation_tx, sort_keys=True)

handcrafted_creation_tx['id'] = handcrafted_creation_tx['id'].encode('utf-8')
prepared_creation_tx['id'] = prepared_creation_tx['id'].encode('utf-8')

```

issue_title: shared_ptr holder constructed but not freed by pybind11
issue_body:
I think I have found a problem with the construction/destruction of smart pointer holders. It seems like pybind11 does not always destruct the smart pointer holders it creates, because it thinks it doesn't "own" them.

Here is a small code snippet that demonstrates that behavior. It registers a class that uses ``std::shared_ptr`` as holder type. As suggested by the pybind11 documentation, ``std::enable_shared_from_this`` is used so that shared pointers and raw pointers can be used interchangeably.

```cpp
	class MyClass : public std::enable_shared_from_this<MyClass> {};
	py::class_<MyClass, std::shared_ptr<MyClass>>(m, "MyClass");

	std::shared_ptr<MyClass> instance = std::make_shared<MyClass>();	
	std::cout << instance.use_count() << std::endl;	// Prints "1", that's the shared_ptr instance above.

	py::object o = py::cast(instance.get());  // Note we are passing a raw pointer to cast()
	std::cout << instance.use_count() << std::endl;	// Prints "2", we now have a 2nd shared_ptr (the holder)

	o = py::none();	// Delete Python variable
	std::cout << instance.use_count() << std::endl;	// Prints "2" (This seems wrong! The holder should have been destroyed)
```

Note that ``cast()`` assumes ``return_value_policy::reference``, because we are passing it a raw pointer. Even though a new ``shared_ptr`` holder is constructed by pybind11 as part of the cast, the ``wrapper->owned`` field is set to ``false`` by ``type_caster_generic::cast()``. This prevents the internal ``shared_ptr`` from getting freed when the wrapper is destroyed. A dangling reference to the C++ class instance remains.

The above problem can be worked around either by passing the ``shared_ptr`` to ``cast()``, not the raw pointer. Or by explicitly passing ``return_value_policy::take_ownership`` to ``cast()`` as second argument.

However, my C++ interface that I am trying to wrap with pybind11 typically returns raw pointers and relies on the ``std::enable_shared_from_this`` mechanism. This allows constructing reference counted pointers from raw pointers only when needed. That means I need ``cast()`` to behave the same for shared pointers and raw pointers. In both cases a ``shared_ptr`` holder should be constructed and, when the Python wrapper goes out of scope, destructed again.

issue_title: Version 1.0.0
issue_body:
## My operating system is (include version):

Ubuntu 17.10

## I installed Certbot with (certbot-auto, OS package manager, pip, etc):

Docker

## I ran this command and it produced this output:

docker run -v /etc/letsencrypt:/etc/letsencrypt -v /var/log/letsencrypt:/var/log/letsencrypt -it certbot/certbot --version

certbot 0.21.1

## Certbot's behavior differed from what I expected because:

Let's Encrypt supports 61 million domains (
https://letsencrypt.org/2017/12/07/looking-forward-to-2018.html
). I don't know what percentage of those use certbot to get their certs, but my wild-ass guess would be >50%.

Software that is used in production by tens of millions of sites should not be marked as "pre-production" software with version 0.x. It's not 100% clear that certbot follows the Semantic Versioning standard, but here's the suggestion from semver.org on when to release 1.0.0 (https://semver.org/#how-do-i-know-when-to-release-100):

> How do I know when to release 1.0.0?

> If your software is being used in production, it should probably already be 1.0.0. If you have a stable > API on which users have come to depend, you should be 1.0.0. If you’re worrying a lot about 
> backwards compatibility, you should probably already be 1.0.0.

## Here is a Certbot log showing the issue (if available):
###### Logs are stored in `/var/log/letsencrypt` by default. Feel free to redact domains, e-mail and IP addresses as you see fit.

No output to the logs, AFAICT

issue_title: Dedupe test code
issue_labels: [{'id': 277080619, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/certbot/certbot/labels/code%20health', 'name': 'code health', 'color': 'fef2c0', 'default': False}, {'id': 149596829, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/certbot/certbot/labels/Good%20Volunteer%20Task', 'name': 'Good Volunteer Task', 'color': 'fef2c0', 'default': False}, {'id': 263357278, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/certbot/certbot/labels/testing', 'name': 'testing', 'color': '7fe57f', 'default': False}]
issue_body:
As pointed out in the discussion of #3972, we have some common patterns in our tests.

#### Pattern 1
`setUp()` creates a temporary directory and `tearDown()` destroys it.

#### Pattern 2
`setUp()` creates a temporary directory and creates/sets `config_dir`, `work_dir`, and/or `logs_dir` paths to be somewhere inside it. `tearDown()` destroys all directories.

Let's dedupe this code. I think the best approach is to make a class in `certbot.tests.util` called something like `TempDirTestCase` for pattern 1 that is a subclass of `unittest.TestCase` and make another class for pattern 2 that is a subclass of `TempDirTestCase`.

issue_title: Constant values in Expr mult and div
issue_body:
Hello,

So `Expr.__mult__` check if the input is a number using `_is_number`, but `Expr.__div__` calls `buildGenExprObj` which check if the object is a number using python builtin `isinstance`:

https://github.com/SCIP-Interfaces/PySCIPOpt/blob/910beeb4b770519c55ec25538258bc3a077bb369/src/pyscipopt/expr.pxi#L115

`_is_number` tries to convert the object to a float and catches the eventual error.
`_is_number` is actually more general than `isinstance`, because it will also catch all the numpy types (np.int, np.float, np.float32...) as numbers.

Is it possible to replace the call to `isinstance(int) or isinstance(float)` with `_is_number`?

Thanks

issue_title: Announce Milestone-0 achievement
Investigate why nodes are joining matrix test channel
issue_body:
## Problem Definition

A lot of Raiden nodes seem to be joining the matrix channel `#test:transport01.raiden.network`. This shouldn't be the case - investigate.

issue_title: Block raiden startup until the ethereum client is fully synchronized
issue_body:
## Problem Definition

Raiden may be connected to an ethereum client that is not fully synchronized, under this circumstance the ethereum client will not provided the latest events required by the Raiden node, this would effectively make Raiden unaware of channel changes and could lead to token loss.

## Solution

Wait for the geth node to fully synchronize before starting to operate.

This is only a proper solution if the Raiden node is starting fresh, i.e. the first run without pending transfers, and it's only as a tool to prevent bad behavior, it's not sufficient for scenarios were the ethereum client had it's database deleted, we cannot prevent that from happening and there is not way to recover aside from wait until the database is synchronized, for this scenario third parties are required to provide safe operation and are outside of the scope of this issue.

## Tasklist

- [ ] On startup query the ethereum node and hold until it's synchronized.

issue_title: Remove partner argument from setTotalWithdraw.
issue_body:
# Problem Definition

The `partner` argument for `setTotalWithdraw` was [removed](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden-contracts/pull/251), this needs to be done in the client as well.

issue_title: Define the maximum number of pending transfers
issue_body:
## Problem Definition

Define the upper limit for the merkle tree size, since the proof is iterate in the `unlock` function.

## Solution

Find what is the maximum length of the merkle proof (thus also the merkle tree height) that can be unlocked with the available gas limit (#438).

## Tasklist

- [x] Write a utility to find the largest tree height, i.e. increase the merkle tree height by one until unlock fails.
- [x] Define this value as a `MAXIMUM_PENDING_TRANSFERS` constant (pending transfers is easier to reason about and to explain to the user in respect to the proof length or tree height).
- [x] Use this maximum height to reject new mediated transfers.
- [x] Write a test that checks the maximum height is enforced.
- [x] Write a test to verify the constant has a sane value.

issue_title: Do not translate variables by default.
issue_body:
I have found it inconvenient to have to put variables such as `ID`, `sample_sheet` etc to `preserved_vars`. In addition, if I would like to write a script specifically for the remote server, I would have to put all variables used in `preserved_vars`.

I think it makes more sense to translate only local targets in `input`, `output`, `depends`, `to_host`, and `from_host`, and leave the rest of the variables untouched by default, because these should have covered most cases when files should be synchronized.

A new option could be named `translate`, or something along the line.

issue_title: Embedded TOC?
issue_body: A TOC seems to be really useful when working with long Jupyter notebooks, yet the toc2 module does not work well with our side panel. It can be helpful to provide native toc support in our side panel, then there is no need to install toc2...

issue_title: Global signature using relative filenames
issue_body: Our signature files keep using relative filenames after they are placed under `~/.sos/.runtime`. They should be converted to absolute filenames to avoid conflicts between projects.

issue_title: Naming convention for path?
issue_body:
Just wondering if we should somehow notify sos the type of variable so that SoS knows what should be translated during remote execution, something like

```
run:
   echo  $(title) >> ${output}
```

or 

```
run:
   echo $${title} >> ${output}
```

or

```
run:
   echo $_{title} >> ${output}
```

instead of

```
task:   preserved_vars='title'
run:
    echo ${title} >> ${output}
```

Of course, `preserved_vars` is much easier to implement than such naming conventions.

issue_title: Multiple global sections?
issue_body:
There is current restriction on there can be only one global section and I do not see a compulsive reason why this should be case. I mean, we can simply join multiple global sections.

In practice, this allows us to define a global section whenever we see a need to define a new global function, and do not have to define everything in the only global section in the script.

```
these are not very useful and should be excluded from HTML report. The same goes to output from standard error, which are supposedly for debug only.

issue_title: unhandy naming of parameter names in SR830 driver (and possibly others)
issue_body:
I would like to comment on a practice I have noticed in a number of places in the qcodes code base I have some comments on. For instance, the SR830 driver has parameters "chN_ratio", "chN_display", "chN_databuffer", where N is in [1, 2]. 

I do not understand why do we not have ch_ratio(N, ....) etc? What happens if in my experiment I want
to switch from channel 1 to 2? Change all numbers manually? Or what happens if I want to access channels one and two? Copy and paste code literally, instead of having a looping construct? Instead of the channel number being part of the parameter name, this should be an argument with which we call the parameter

This happens not only in this driver but in a number of them, and I do not think we should work this way.  What do you guys think?

issue_title: Cannot load pretrained InceptionResnetV2
issue_body:
I tried to load pretrained InceptionResnetV2 by:
`learn = ConvLearner.pretrained(arch, data)` 
where arch = inceptionresnet_2, but I got the following error:

> KeyError: unexpected key 'classif.bias' fastai in state_dict'

I checked and there is a a mismatching (module `torch.imports` vs `inceptionresnetv2 ` class) as followings:

https://github.com/fastai/fastai/blob/master/fastai/torch_imports.py#L65
which uses inceptionresnetv2-d579a627

https://github.com/fastai/fastai/blob/master/fastai/models/inceptionresnetv2.py#L8
which uses inceptionresnetv2-520b38e4

I replaced d579a627 with 520b38e4 (also download its pretrained weights) and it works.

issue_title: Add `-g` shorthand for `pulumi new`'s `--generate-only` flag
Creating a resource ended up with an empty parent
Java and Python `UniqueID` generation does not match
issue_body:
### Describe the problem
The Java and Python frontends currently use different algorithms to generate `TaskID`s and `ObjectID`s. Ideally, **they should both use the functions in `src/ray/id.h` to generate `UniqueID`s**. This is the code that is currently shared between the Python frontend and the Raylet backend.

This problem manifests itself when the Raylet backend tries to compute the `TaskID` of the task that generated a given `ObjectID`. It does this, for instance, when trying to decide whether to reconstruct an object. As of #2526, the exact sequence of steps is:
1. The `ReconstructionPolicy` is asked to reconstruct an `object_id`, which was computed with the code in `java/runtime-common/src/main/java/org/ray/core/UniqueIdHelper.java`.
2. The `ReconstructionPolicy` computes the task ID from `object_id`, but gets the wrong task ID, since it uses the `ComputeTaskId` helper function in `src/ray/id.h` to do so. It begins to listen for task lease notifications for the wrong task ID.
3. The `ReconstructionPolicy` soon times out the task since no notifications were received and attempts to reconstruct the task. However, it can't find the `TaskSpec` since it has the wrong task ID.

issue_body:
### System information

- **Ray version**: Unknown (cannot run `ray.__version__` yet). Source was cloned from https://github.com/ray-project/ray.git on June 4, 2018.
- **Python version**: Python 3.6.0 Anaconda custom (x86_64)

### Describe the problem
I followed all the Mac OS dependency setup instructions on the readthedocs.io page to build Ray from source (http://ray.readthedocs.io/en/latest/installation.html). Additionally, I had to:

before I was able to get building Ray from source to run to its entirety and see the message `Successfully installed ray.`  

However, I'm currently stuck on getting the line `import ray` to work (I also get this error when trying the run the test/runtest.py script to test if my Ray installation succeeded). In both jupyter and when running python from console, I get this error which seems to suggest an issue with my pyarrow installation:

I first thought it was an issue with the pyarrow installed on my overall machine, since initially, I had also received the same error message when I tested trying to get python to `import pyarrow` directly (and not importing pyarrow through ray). 
- **I tried reverting** my overall machine's installation to a previous version of pyarrow, pyarrow version 0.8.0 (as suggested in a reply in this archived email https://www.mail-archive.com/search?l=issues@arrow.apache.org&q=subject:%22%5C%5Bjira%5C%5D+%5C%5BCommented%5C%5D+%5C%28ARROW%5C-2355%5C%29+%5C%5BPython%5C%5D+Unable+to+import+pyarrow+%5C%5B0.9.0%5C%5D+OSX%22&o=newest&f=1 as a workaround), but that didn't work.

issue_title: When a worker dies while executing a task, the worker's log file should be fetched.
issue_body:
Right now the user may see an error message like

```
A worker died or was killed while executing a task.

  You can inspect errors by running

      ray.error_info()

  If this driver is hanging, start a new one with

      ray.init(redis_address="10.98.246.67:6379")
```

This error message would be more useful if it included any of the following:
1. The IP address of the machine that the worker was on, plus the names of the worker's log files.
2. A command for retrieving the log files for the worker that died.
3. The (possibly truncated) logfiles for the worker that died.

For an example use case, this would help debug #1479.

issue_title: Correct way to implement a cancel button w/ UpdateView+ModelForm?
issue_body:
Just wanted to run this by you and get your thoughts... If you're using ModelForms and UpdateView, you're letting Django take care of the form layout, tweaking some setting perhaps via Crispy, and then letting Crispy render it. In these types of views, you often want to have a Cancel button on your form: ![pastedgraphic-1](https://f.cloud.github.com/assets/1718983/1141192/0f5cceb0-1caf-11e3-8a62-6258a0078583.png) If you Google around, end users of Crispy don't seem to have a consistent idea of how to accomplish this. *\* Some people suggest appending raw HTML via Crispy's helper object -- not a clean approach *\* Some people suggest just using Crispy to render the fields, then wrap your own form in HTML -- not clean either, since now you've got to maintain code in two places *\* Some people suggest putting a button in which has onclick="history.go(-1)" -- go back a page in the browser history. The history(-1) approach is bad if you submit with bad data, which posts and comes back invalid, and then try to cancel -- in this case, going back one page takes you back to the form you submitted with invalid data. (As an aside, Django is supposed to be Javascript-agnostic, meaning that if you've got to resort to DOM manipulation via Javascript to accomplish something this basic, you're probably doing something wrong.) I had an idea - perhaps you could add another Submit button, this time labeled "Cancel", and style it accordingly. basically, taking this: ``` self.helper.add_input(Submit('submit', 'Create Project')) ``` and making it this: ``` self.helper.add_input(Submit('submit', 'Create Project')) self.helper.add_input(Submit('cancel', 'Cancel', css_class='btn-default')) ``` (The styling on the Cancel button was supposed to give me a basic black button as appears in my picture above.) Since these are both form submits which do a post back to the view, your view code would look something like this. ``` def form_valid(self, form): if 'cancel' in self.request.POST: return HttpResponseRedirect(self.get_success_url()) return super(Project_UpdateView, self).form_valid(form) ``` Basically, seeing if 'cancel' is in the POST dictionary and redirecting accordingly, otherwise following the normal path. It ends up functioning, but I noticed that I had very little control over the styling of that new Cancel button. If I styled it "btn-danger" (i.e. Red button), that worked. Otherwise, it always seemed to end up Blue like the other button. ![pastedgraphic-2](https://f.cloud.github.com/assets/1718983/1141201/41edd888-1caf-11e3-9e4c-11a841b3b9ea.png) So I looked into the Submit class within Crispy and found this: ``` input_type = 'submit' field_classes = 'submit submitButton' if TEMPLATE_PACK == 'uni_form' else 'btn btn-primary' ``` What this means is that if you're using bootstrap it's always going to get styled the same way ('btn-primary', i.e. Blue button). Due to how bootstrap works, the Red danger button overrides this style, but things like Default button don't. My solution: create a new SubmitButton class which removes the "btn-primary" styling. from django.conf import settings TEMPLATE_PACK = getattr(settings, 'CRISPY_TEMPLATE_PACK', 'bootstrap') class SubmitButton(BaseInput): input_type = 'submit' field_classes = 'button' if TEMPLATE_PACK == 'uni_form' else 'btn' #removed btn-primary When you use it, you've got to specify the style of the button, since the "btn-primary" default is gone now. So now, when you use it in Crispy layout code, it's clean and simple and you now have control over the styling of your buttons. ``` self.helper.add_input(SubmitButton('submit', 'Update',css_class='btn-primary')) self.helper.add_input(SubmitButton('cancel', 'Cancel',css_class='btn btn-default')) ``` and the rendered HTML is correct: ```
``` There's probably a better solution which would involve adding an anchor, which is either styled like a button or wraps a button, and links back to the page which invoked the UpdateView in the first place. The anchor approach would mean one less transaction (the POST) to handle the Cancel operation.

issue_title: `DatePicker` should work with `datetime.date` instead of `datetime.datetime`
issue_body:
A `DatePicker` really is about selecting a date and should probably yield a `datetime.date` value. It it implemented by a [`<input type=date>`](https://www.w3.org/TR/html5/forms.html#date-state-(type=date)) which is supposed to convey a [date](https://www.w3.org/TR/html5/infrastructure.html#concept-date). 

Currently `DatePicker` yields a `datetime.datetime`:

~~~
@widgets.interact(x=widgets.DatePicker())
def foo(x):
    return x
=> datetime.datetime(2017, 5, 19, 0, 0)
~~~

It would be nice to be able to have `DatePicker` work on `datetime.date` (as an option ?).

issue_title: ImportError with html5lib
issue_body:
Looks like `html5lib` has been introduced as a depency at: https://github.com/python-mechanize/mechanize/commit/75f3690d12b793e05fc118b9944c6753a0dc8a7a

This is easy to reproduce:

```
>>> import mechanize
>>> br = mechanize.Browser()
>>> br.open('https://example.com')
>>> br.select_form('whatever')
```
An easy workaround is just to install the missing library with: `pip install html5lib`. However, I would expect that such internal dependency would be already installed along mechanize.

What about adding the following to `setup()` at `setup.py`?

```
install_requires=['html5lib']
```
Or with a fix version:

```
install_requires=['html5lib==0.999999999']
```

issue_title: big picture & beyond Python
issue_body:
I see the benefit of the nursery and cancellation control structures presented by Nathaniel.  Also Trio's stance of avoiding threading, along with merging scheduling and cancellation concepts makes sense.  Relegating all concurrency interaction to cooperative, explicit switching within a single thread seems to be a clear sweet spot for Python given the GIL and CPU-bound threading issues.

What I'm trying to reconcile is how to apply these concepts elsewhere.  For example, Lua doesn't have a GIL issue.  So do we borrow the nursery and cancellation, but allow full threading (and associated headaches) to enable scaling to multiple cores?  Or would limiting preemptive multitasking to the equivalent of run_sync_in_worker_thread() be sufficient for 99% of use cases?  Or how about allowing individual threads to each run their own Trio event loop with all state thread-local, and somehow have apps carefully dispatch work to each thread?

Another question:  how well can a library like Trio be implemented in languages lacking colored function support?  (E.g. Lua or D language)

issue_title: Nervous about the auto-upload-to-pypi feature
issue_body:
I'm nervous about your use of Travis's auto-upload-to-pypi feature.

The problem is that right now, anyone who has write access to this repo can upload arbitrary packages to pypi using your username/password, without telling anyone or generating any notifications at all -- all they have to do is directly push a commit to this repo under some new branch.

I'm not sure, but they might also be able to directly read out your pypi password and then do whatever they want with it.

I don't think anyone will *actually* do this, but we have a [very liberal policy for handing out write access](https://trio.readthedocs.io/en/latest/contributing.html#joining-the-team), and part of the idea for making that work is to make sure that what we're actually handing out is just the ability to merge pull requests, so even if someone did decide to be evil then they'd still have to do that by submitting an evil PR, which would send everyone notifications and we'd roll it back immediately. But this doesn't work for the auto-pypi feature. (I *really* wish github had the ability to entirely disable direct pushes to a repo, but alas it does not.)

Anyway, this means that right now there are 10 people with write access to this repo = 10 people who you may have just given your pypi password to... maybe it would be better to disable that feature and change your pypi password?

issue_title: Naming maxThreads/max_threads/max_workers
issue_body: The class definition for the ThreadPoolExecutor uses `max_workers` as a kwarg. The config options for threads specify `maxThreads`. We need to update all of these to use `max_threads` to be consistent going forward.

Major `rdopkg` actions (`fix`, `patch`, `new-version`) now have [human readable feature tests/descriptions](https://github.com/softwarefactory-project/rdopkg/tree/master/features).

issue_title: Output tensor type for size op (in node/test_size)
issue_body:
Hi -- 
There seems to be an issue with the exact type of output TensorProtos in at least one node test. Signed vs unsigned. 

in directory node/test_size/test_data_set_0:

```
import onnx
f_out = open("output_0.pb", "rb")
new_tensor = onnx.TensorProto()
new_tensor.ParseFromString(f_out.read())
```
At this point `new_tensor.data_type` is 7. Looking in onnx.pb.h I see, 
```
TensorProto_DataType_INT64 = 7,
```
(aside : is there a function to get a string like `"signed long"` from an enum type in Python ONNX API)?

However the Size Operator spec specifies UINT64 (`13`) as output. So I'd expect the type of the tensor loaded from `output_0.pb` here to be `13` and not `7`.

issue_title: os.cpu_count()
issue_body: `--workers auto` uses `os.cpu_count()` which is in Python 3.4 or newer, whereas `multiprocessing.cpu_count()` is in Python 2.6 or newer.

issue_title: rdflib.store.Memory not working with python 2.7
issue_body:
imrantau...@gmail.com, 2011-09-14T20:12:38.000Z 
What steps will reproduce the problem?
1.from rdflib.store.Memory import Memory
2.it gives error mentioned below
3.from rdflib.store.Memory import Memory
ImportError: No module named Memory

What is the expected output? What do you see instead?
rdflib.store.Memory should work

What version of the product are you using? On what operating system?
python 2.7

Please provide any additional information below.

Comment 1 by gjhigg...@gmail.com
The supported approach is to use the "IOMemory" store via the plugin mechanism. 

Below is shown the reasonably complete example on the wiki's IntroStore page (http://code.google.com/p/rdflib/wiki/IntroStore) that has been straightforwardly adapted to use IOMemory and is ready for copy'n'pasta. HTH

{{{
from rdflib import ConjunctiveGraph as Graph
from rdflib import plugin
from rdflib.store import Store, NO_STORE, VALID_STORE
from rdflib import Namespace
from rdflib import Literal
from rdflib import URIRef

default_graph_uri = "http://rdflib.net/rdfstore"
configString = ""

# Get the mysql plugin. You may have to install the python mysql libraries
store = plugin.get('IOMemory', Store)('rdfstore')

# Open previously created store, or create it if it doesn't exist yet
rt = store.open(configString,create=False)
if rt == NO_STORE:
    # There is no underlying MySQL infrastructure, create it
    store.open(configString,create=True)
else:
    assert rt == VALID_STORE,"There underlying store is corrupted"
    
# There is a store, use it
graph = Graph(store, identifier = URIRef(default_graph_uri))

print "Triples in graph before add: ", len(graph)

# Now we'll add some triples to the graph & commit the changes
rdflibns = Namespace('http://rdflib.net/test/')
graph.add((rdflibns['pic:1'], rdflibns['name'], Literal('Jane & Bob')))
graph.add((rdflibns['pic:2'], rdflibns['name'], Literal('Squirrel in Tree')))
graph.commit()

print "Triples in graph after add: ", len(graph)

# display the graph in RDF/XML
print graph.serialize() 
}}}


Comment 2 by gjhigg...@gmail.com
And there are more examples of using graphs and stores in the API docs:

http://bel-epa.com/rdflibapidocs/rdflib.graph-module.html


Comment 3 by gjhigg...@gmail.com
I'll handle this one.


Comment 4 by imrantau...@gmail.com
Thanks, that works.


Comment 5 by gjhigg...@gmail.com
Ah, (a little later) I see. It's been refactored.

For your original approach you'd need:

from rdflib.plugins.memory import Memory

Sorry it took so long for the penny to drop.

HTH


Graham.


Comment 6 by gjhigg...@gmail.com
No further action required.

3.0.0
